stories_covered: Chime IPO filing at $1.67B revenue, 8.6M users
Late-stage pricing and ratchet protections in IPOs
Public vs private market capital costs
harry_takes: Chime's IPO timing is smart despite recent market volatility; market windows don't stay open forever
Late-stage companies that prefer private markets are forgoing cheaper capital because they don't understand their true cost
Venture makes embarrassingly large percentage of returns in rare 'must acquire' moments; the other six years are about keeping portfolio alive
Death of IPOs is bad for capital allocation; public markets allow founders and LPs to make independent liquidity choices
harry_boldest_quote: We make an embarrassingly large percentage of our money once every seven years when you're in the white heat of must acquire must own high growth venture assets.
predictions: Lemkin: OpenAI will eventually stop being a nonprofit (through PBC structure with Microsoft/investors), but bureaucracy will delay resolution
Panel consensus: ChatGPT 5 likely revealed in 2025; velocity of release is so high it wouldn't surprise anyone
Stebbings: Rippling will prevail in Deal lawsuit; counterclaims are sign of losing party looking for settlement offsets
Panel: More billion-dollar revenue companies should IPO now; market window is open but won't stay that way
disagreements: Lemkin thinks OpenAI's nonprofit structure will persist due to board power dynamics; Stebbings argues lawyers will eventually untangle it because asset value is too high. Jason skeptical on venture 'fun returner' returns; panel agrees they're insufficient.
harry_role: Harry drives the conversation with strong takes on IPO timing, late-stage economics, and regulatory issues. He frames larger questions about venture returns and market structure. Moderates debate between Lemkin and O'Driscoll while also contributing his own analysis.
notable: Extended debate on why venture exits are growing so large (Google example: would be $140B if delayed one year) and how that concentrates opportunity in mega-funds; discussion of tax incentives for holding vs selling private companies.
